title: VA Accelerates Cloud Migration With $2.3B AI Infrastructure Investment
text: The Department of Veterans Affairs announced Thursday a $2.3 billion investment to accelerate its cloud migration and integrate artificial intelligence capabilities across its healthcare delivery system. The five-year modernization plan aims to transition 78% of legacy systems to cloud infrastructure by fiscal year 2028, up from the current 34%. VA Deputy Secretary Tanya Bradsher told reporters the initiative will reduce IT operational costs by an estimated $440 million annually while improving veteran access to care. The department plans to deploy AI-powered diagnostic tools in 172 VA medical centers by Q4 2026, starting with radiology and pathology departments. Early pilot programs at three facilities showed a 23% reduction in diagnostic turnaround time and a 15% improvement in early detection rates for certain cancers. The investment includes $680 million for cybersecurity enhancements and zero-trust architecture implementation. Bradsher emphasized that all AI systems will undergo rigorous testing for bias and accuracy before deployment. The VA expects to hire 850 cloud engineers and data scientists over the next 18 months to support the transition, with hiring prioritizing veterans and individuals with disabilities.

title: Treasury Department Reports 340% Surge in Ransomware Attacks on Federal Contractors
text: Federal contractors experienced a 340% increase in ransomware attacks during fiscal year 2025 compared to the previous year, according to a Treasury Department report released Wednesday. The analysis, which examined 1,847 reported incidents across 412 contractor organizations, found that attackers successfully encrypted data in 62% of cases, with average ransom demands reaching $4.7 million. Assistant Secretary for Cyber Policy Rebecca Chen stated that the spike reflects increasingly sophisticated attacks targeting supply chain vulnerabilities. The report revealed that 89% of successful breaches originated from compromised credentials rather than technical exploits. In response, the Office of Management and Budget issued new guidance requiring all federal contractors handling sensitive data to implement multifactor authentication and endpoint detection by September 2026. Organizations failing to comply will be ineligible for contract renewals. The Treasury estimates compliance costs at $125,000 to $380,000 per organization, depending on size and current security posture. Chen noted that while the upfront investment is substantial, the average cost of a successful ransomware attack for government contractors exceeded $8.2 million when factoring in recovery, notification, and lost business. The report recommends Congress allocate $500 million in grants to help small contractors meet the new security requirements.

title: Federal Agencies Face 18% Attrition Rate as Private Sector Poaches Tech Talent
text: Federal agencies are losing technology workers at an unprecedented rate, with attrition reaching 18.3% in fiscal year 2025, more than double the government-wide average of 7.8%, according to Office of Personnel Management data analyzed by the Partnership for Public Service. The exodus is particularly acute among cybersecurity professionals and software engineers, where private sector salaries often exceed federal pay by 45% to 65%. The General Services Administration reported losing 127 of its 340-person Technology Transformation Services staff last year, forcing delays in several cross-agency modernization initiatives. OPM Director Kiran Ahuja told a Senate hearing Tuesday that the administration is proposing expanded use of direct hire authority and student loan repayment programs up to $80,000 per employee for critical tech positions. The proposal also includes creating a new cyber excepted service that would allow agencies to offer salaries up to $255,800 for senior cybersecurity roles, bypassing traditional GS pay scales. A pilot program at the Department of Homeland Security offering fully remote work arrangements and flexible schedules reduced tech worker attrition from 21% to 9% over 18 months. Ahuja emphasized that without legislative action to modernize federal compensation structures, agencies will struggle to maintain the technical expertise needed for ongoing digital transformation efforts.

title: House Appropriations Committee Proposes 12% Cut to Federal IT Modernization Fund
text: The House Appropriations Committee released draft legislation Wednesday that would reduce funding for the Technology Modernization Fund from $500 million to $440 million for fiscal year 2027, despite administration requests for a $750 million increase. Committee Chairman Robert Williams defended the cut, stating that improved project management and cost controls should enable agencies to accomplish more with less. The TMF has awarded $3.2 billion across 87 projects since its inception, with the General Services Administration reporting an average return on investment of 340% over five years through operational savings and improved service delivery. However, a recent GAO audit found that 23% of funded projects experienced significant delays or cost overruns, with eight projects terminated after spending a combined $156 million. Federal CIO Clare Martorana warned that the proposed cut would force the cancellation of 12 planned modernization projects and delay critical legacy system replacements at the Social Security Administration and IRS. The draft bill does include $180 million in new funding for a Federal AI Infrastructure Initiative and maintains the $65 million annual appropriation for the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency's continuous diagnostics and mitigation program. Williams noted that the committee expects to fund efficiency gains through the proposed cut, stating that past performance suggests room for improved execution before increasing appropriations.

title: NASA-SpaceX Partnership Delivers $1.8B in Cost Savings Through Reusable Launch Systems
text: NASA's decade-long partnership with SpaceX has generated $1.8 billion in cost savings compared to traditional launch procurement methods, according to an independent analysis released by the space agency Monday. The study examined 47 cargo and crew missions to the International Space Station between 2020 and 2026, finding that reusable Falcon 9 and Dragon spacecraft reduced per-mission costs by 58% compared to earlier expendable vehicle programs. NASA Administrator Janet Petro stated that the public-private partnership model has fundamentally transformed space economics while maintaining a perfect safety record across 23 crewed flights. The analysis showed average launch costs declined from $152 million per mission using traditional methods to $64 million with commercial crew vehicles. The savings enabled NASA to redirect $890 million toward Artemis lunar program development and $420 million toward Mars Sample Return mission acceleration. The partnership also spurred broader commercial space industry growth, with SpaceX reporting 340 total launches in 2025, including 89 missions for commercial customers and 28 for Department of Defense. However, the report noted dependency risks, recommending NASA maintain relationships with multiple commercial providers. Boeing's Starliner program, while experiencing delays, provides backup crew transport capability. Petro emphasized that the model demonstrates how strategic partnerships can drive innovation while reducing taxpayer costs, with lessons applicable to other federal procurement domains.
